content:
Sharing an important AFR judgment of the Hon’ble High Court of Juridcature at Patna in which I had the privilege of appearing for the petitioner.

A Division Bench of the Patna High Court comprising Hon’ble the Acting Chief Justice Mr. Justice Sudhir Singh and Hon’ble Mr. Justice Rajesh Kumar Verma has delivered a significant judgment clarifying the legal limits of debarment in public procurement.

Placing reliance on the Supreme Court’s decision in Kulja Industries Ltd. v. Chief General Manager, Western Telecom Project, BSNL, (2014) 14 SCC 731, while reiterating the settled principles emerging from Erusian Equipment & Chemicals Ltd. v. State of West Bengal and the subsequent line of authorities, the Court held that once the contractual work has been completed, accepted by the employer, and a work experience certificate has been issued, the very foundation of the debarment ceases to exist. The Court further held that permitting such debarment to continue thereafter would, in effect, amount to an impermissible indefinite debarment, offending the doctrine of proportionality. Consequently, the debarment order was set aside.

The judgment makes a valuable contribution to the jurisprudence governing blacklisting and debarment, reaffirming that although the State possesses the inherent power to debar contractors, such power must always be exercised fairly, reasonably and proportionately, and cannot continue after the very basis for its exercise has ceased to exist.

It is always a privilege to be part of matters that contribute to the development and clarification of the law.
